What percentage of small-scale food prod.content: A   T ANNUAL REPORT  •  Please use this section to provide a brief summary of the progress of the project(s) during the  reporting period and a summary of the monitoring information obtained.  ll project activities were carried out as per the activity plan set for the plantation.   There have been continuous agronomic operations to ensure the maintenance of 1,018  •  ha of cultivated Jatropha   To ensure optimum growth of the plants 318 tons of N:P: K (20:10:10) fertilizer was  •  applied to the plantation.    Recurrent  weeds  that  compete  with  and  suppress  the  plantation  were  controlled  by  •  manual  and  mechanical  means.  In  addition  to  slashing,  5640  liters  of  weedicide  were  applied to control the weeds.   Pest scouting teams were deployed throughout the year to monitor the occurrence of  •  pests and diseases. Depending on the type and extent of pests and diseases appropriate  control measures were taken.   Plant protective products were used whenever required to control disease and pests.  •  Accordingly, a total of 576 and 3.366 liters of disease and insect pest control  products, respectively, were used during the reporting period.   A total of 469.2 tons of fruit was harvested during the reporting port period. The yield  •  was not satisfactory due to the apparent drought spell during the growing season.  The project has also made significant improvements by increasing the performance of  •  the assigned SDG indicators (SDGs 8,13,15).   To address SDG 8, health screening for 150 people in the project area.   •  SDG 8 has also seen stability in employee enrollment with 60% permanent workers and  •  40% temporary workers, with workers’ average daily earnings (ADE) rising from 13.43  GHC  to  14.37  GHC  over  the  period  (ADE  figures  for  2022  period  has  been  corrected  from the already published Annual report version 2.0, 10/12/2022).  Community  development  projects/support  systems  also  took  place  e.g.,  support  for  •  community festivals, farmers’ day celebrations, etc.  SDG 15 ensured consistent training on biodiversity conservation.   •  The  are  1,536  indigenous  trees  retained  in  the  plantation  to  conserve  the  ecosystem  •  and biodiversity of the area by creating conducive niches for essential insects, birds and  other animals of the ecosystem. A   T ANNUAL REPORT  •  Please use this section to provide a brief summary of the progress of the project(s) during the  reporting period and a summary of the monitoring information obtained.  ll project activities were carried out as per the activity plan set for the plantation.   There have been continuous agronomic operations to ensure the maintenance of 1,018  •  ha of cultivated Jatropha   To ensure optimum growth of the plants 318 tons of N:P: K (20:10:10) fertilizer was  •  applied to the plantation.    Recurrent  weeds  that  compete  with  and  suppress  the  plantation  were  controlled  by  •  manual  and  mechanical  means.  In  addition  to  slashing,  5640  liters  of  weedicide  were  applied to control the weeds.   Pest scouting teams were deployed throughout the year to monitor the occurrence of  •  pests and diseases. Depending on the type and extent of pests and diseases appropriate  control measures were taken.   Plant protective products were used whenever required to control disease and pests.  •  Accordingly, a total of 576 and 3.366 liters of disease and insect pest control  products, respectively, were used during the reporting period.   A total of 469.2 tons of fruit was harvested during the reporting port period. The yield  •  was not satisfactory due to the apparent drought spell during the growing season.  The project has also made significant improvements by increasing the performance of  •  the assigned SDG indicators (SDGs 8,13,15). According to the CFS methodology - Forest Inventory Guideline under CarbonFix Standard, the plot size  should be large enough so that at least 10 trees (ideal is 12 to 15 trees) are measured within the plots  boundaries.   2.  MAI unit is “t of aboveground and belowground dry biomass”.   3.  The permanent plots were randomly selected. The plantation boundaries or blocks were open on the  map and randomly picked the coordinates without any bias. After that the center of the plot was set  according to random coordinates.   4.  According to the lease land agreements, JOil leased the agriculture land for 6,500 hectares and only  4,500 hectares can be developed for the Jatropha plantations. Hence, the remaining 2,000 hectares are  conserved for farmer displacement. Furthermore, the land with 6,500 hectares has been zoned as an  agricultural area hence the plantations development and displaced farming does not conflict with the  land use. Therefore, farmers' displacement (displace from 4,500 hectares boundaries to 2,000 hectares)  does not cause leakage as the land use in baseline and project scenario are similar.   5.  There is a mistake not including the formula to PDD. However, the formulas in section D.4 have been  provided in PDD version 04.

Therefore, the aboveground dry biomass is the aboveground fresh biomass multiply by  ratio dry to fresh (0.30).  8. Quality education.summary: The evidence from the Human Resources Management Plan (SOIL/ESMS/MP/HR01) (Document: GS11154_JOil_AR_PDD_v05.3_final_clean.pdf, Page: 69) mentions that the project prevents discrimination in hiring, access to training, promotion, and retirement on the grounds of gender. However, this does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Number of training hours provided for employees (full-time, part-time, or temporary), disaggregated per gender'. The project monitors average hourly earnings of all female and male employees, by occupation, age, and disability (Document: GS11154_JOil_AR_MR_v03.2_trackchange.pdf, Page: 23), but this does not directly indicate access to training opportunities. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

This is a qualitative statement and does not provide explicit quantitative data. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

Finally, with the resulting raster information, a post-processing was carried out, which 
consisted of changing the projection system of the WGS84 geographic coordinate

system to the flat WGS84-UTM 30N system, transforming the resulting layer (raster) to 
vector format, eliminating isolated polygons smaller than 1 ha, constructing the table 
of attributes with the following categories: eligible and non-eligible, and cutting the 
layer to the boundaries of the project area.

8. Decent work and economic growth.summary: The evidence provided does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Total number of employees earning above local minimum wage'. Although the local minimum wage is mentioned, the average daily earnings (ADE) for employees are provided, but not explicitly linked to the local minimum wage. The percentage of employees earning above the minimum wage is calculated, but based on indirect assumptions. Therefore, the evidence is treated conservatively, and the score is 0.

Decent work and economic growth.summary: The project's Human Resources Management Plan (SOIL/ESMS/MP/HR01) prevents discrimination in hiring, access to training, promotion, and retirement on gender. However, the project does not provide explicit evidence of its impact on gender disparities in employment. The project's net benefit is estimated to be 16.47% for female employees and 12.27% for male employees, but this is relative to a scenario with no job creation. The evidence does not directly measure the indicator variable, which requires the total number of work hours created, disaggregated by gender.

8. Decent work and economic growth.summary: The evidence from the project documents (GS11154_JOil_AR_PDD_v05.3_final_clean.pdf, Page: 93; GS11154_JOil_AR_MR_v03.2_trackchange.pdf, Page: 74; GS11154_JOil_AR_VER+report_v1.5_final_clean.pdf, Page: 3) suggests that the project aims to achieve economic growth through the creation of productive employment opportunities. However, the evidence is qualitative and does not provide explicit quantitative data to directly measure income generation from other activities, such as ecotourism. The project mentions increasing income of families involved with the project, but this is not directly related to the indicator variable. Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

Therefore, the evidence does not satisfy the indicator's measurement requirement.

Water buffers 
The project will create water buffers between plantation and water bodies to protect the 
soil along the water bodies.
Buffers also reduce the risk that the plantation will be lost 
if the course of the river changes.

13. Climate action.summary: The project's evidence satisfies the indicator's measurement requirement by providing explicit quantitative data on the amount of CO2 removed by its activities. The project estimates its total CO2 certificates for the project area to be 2,201 tCO2eq (Document: GS11154_JOil_AR_VER+report_v1.5_final_clean.pdf, Page: 23). This data directly measures the indicator variable, 'Amount of GHGs emissions avoided or sequestered'. The evidence is based on the Gold Standard Afforestation/Reforestation (A/R) GHG Emissions Reduction & Sequestration Methodology, version 1, which is a validated/verified emission reductions unit.

15. Life on land.summary: The evidence provided does not directly measure the indicator variable 'Forest areas managed sustainably for forest products including sustainable produced fuelwood'. The total area of forest land under sustainable management practices is mentioned as 2,862.97 ha, but this is not directly related to the indicator variable. The project's focus on conservation and biodiversity management, and the implementation of sustainable forest management practices such as the establishment of Jatropha plantations, are relevant but qualitative only. The evidence does not contain explicit quantitative data that directly measures or clearly supports the indicator. (Document: GS11154_JOil_AR_PDD_v05.3_final_clean.pdf, Page 19, 102, 110, 120)

A   T ANNUAL REPORT  •  Please use this section to provide a brief summary of the progress of the project(s) during the  reporting period and a summary of the monitoring information obtained.  ll project activities were carried out as per the activity plan set for the plantation.   There have been continuous agronomic operations to ensure the maintenance of 1,018  •  ha of cultivated Jatropha   To ensure optimum growth of the plants 318 tons of N:P: K (20:10:10) fertilizer was  •  applied to the plantation.    Recurrent  weeds  that  compete  with  and  suppress  the  plantation  were  controlled  by  •  manual  and  mechanical  means.  In  addition  to  slashing,  5640  liters  of  weedicide  were  applied to control the weeds.   Pest scouting teams were deployed throughout the year to monitor the occurrence of  •  pests and diseases. Depending on the type and extent of pests and diseases appropriate  control measures were taken.   Plant protective products were used whenever required to control disease and pests.  •  Accordingly, a total of 576 and 3.366 liters of disease and insect pest control  products, respectively, were used during the reporting period.   A total of 469.2 tons of fruit was harvested during the reporting port period. The yield  •  was not satisfactory due to the apparent drought spell during the growing season.  The project has also made significant improvements by increasing the performance of  •  the assigned SDG indicators (SDGs 8,13,15).   To address SDG 8, health screening for 150 people in the project area.   •  SDG 8 has also seen stability in employee enrollment with 60% permanent workers and  •  40% temporary workers, with workers’ average daily earnings (ADE) rising from 13.43  GHC  to  14.37  GHC  over  the  period  (ADE  figures  for  2022  period  has  been  corrected  from the already published Annual report version 2.0, 10/12/2022).  Community  development  projects/support  systems  also  took  place  e.g.,  support  for  •  community festivals, farmers’ day celebrations, etc.  SDG 15 ensured consistent training on biodiversity conservation.   •  The  are  1,536  indigenous  trees  retained  in  the  plantation  to  conserve  the  ecosystem  •  and biodiversity of the area by creating conducive niches for essential insects, birds and  other animals of the ecosystem.   Stakeholder  engagements  between  chiefs,  opinion  leaders,  and  •  departments  within  the  communities  and  districts  of  operations  are  harmonized  to  receive continued feedback on the project e.g. Ghana Fire Service, Ministry of Food and  Agriculture, National Disaster Management Organization, and District Health directorate.   Training and capacity building have been carried out for workers on health and safety,  •  biodiversity conservation, and fire management as indicated in the following table.  able 1.0 Training and Capacity Building:  | : S/N | : TOPICS | : Number  of  participants | : Date  of  training | : Remarks | government:
